title: Cosine Similarity on TF-IDF
concept: By converting documents to TF-IDF vectors, we can calculate the cosine of the angle between them to measure semantic similarity, ignoring length.

exercises.prompt: Which mathematical tool do we use on TF-IDF vectors to find how close their topics are, regardless of document length?
exercises.explain: TF-IDF turns text into lists of numbers (vectors) based on word importance. Cosine similarity then calculates the angle between these vectors to see how similar they are.

cards.title: Comparing Apples to Big Apple Orchards
cards.body: How does a search engine know that a short news headline and a long blog post are talking about the exact same story? It doesn't just count matching words. It looks at the direction of their vocabulary! Let's learn how math helps us compare texts of any size.

cards.title: What is Cosine Similarity?
cards.body: Cosine Similarity is a metric used to measure how similar two text documents are, regardless of their size. It calculates the cosine of the angle between two word vectors projected in a multi-dimensional space. By using TF-IDF vectors, it focuses on the direction of the vectors (representing word patterns) rather than their length.

cards.title: The Flashlight Beam Analogy
cards.body: Imagine pointing two flashlights in a dark room. If they point in the exact same direction, the angle between them is zero, and they are perfectly aligned (similarity of 1). If they point in completely different directions, they don't overlap at all. It doesn't matter if one flashlight is super bright (a long document) and the other is dim (a short document); we only care about the direction they are pointing!

cards.title: Recipe Matchmaker
cards.body: Suppose we compare two cooking documents. Document A says: 'Recipe: Bake bread with yeast and flour.' Document B says: 'How to bake delicious homemade sourdough bread using flour and yeast.' Even though Document B is longer, their TF-IDF vectors point in nearly the same direction because they share key terms like 'yeast', 'flour', and 'bake'. The cosine similarity score will be very close to 1!

cards.title: The Length Myth
cards.body: Users often think that longer documents will automatically score higher in similarity matches. This is a myth! Cosine similarity normalizes for length. It only looks at the angle between the vectors, meaning a short tweet and a long article can have a perfect similarity score if they use the same balance of unique words.

cards.title: Angles Over Length
cards.body: See how document length doesn't change the direction of the vector. We only look at the angle between them!

left.text: Vectors point in a very similar direction. High similarity score (close to 1).

right.text: One vector is much longer (more words), but the angle between them remains the same.

exercises.prompt: What is the highest possible Cosine Similarity score, representing two documents that point in the exact same direction?
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity values range from 0 (completely different/orthogonal vectors) to 1 (identical direction/highest similarity) for positive TF-IDF vectors.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Cosine similarity allows us to compare a short tweet to a long essay without the essay's length bias ruining the score.
exercises.explain: True! Because cosine similarity only measures the angle (direction) between vectors, it is completely unaffected by how long the documents are.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how we measure similarity between document vectors.
exercises.sentence: Cosine similarity calculates the cosine of the [blank] between two vectors.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two TF-IDF vectors. The smaller the angle, the more similar the documents are!

exercises.prompt: Identify the statement about Cosine Similarity that contains AI-generated misinformation:

options.text: Cosine similarity projects documents as vectors in space and measures the angle between them to find similarity.
Cosine similarity is not useful for comparing long and short documents because longer documents automatically get higher scores.
Using TF-IDF with cosine similarity helps us ignore common filler words and focus on unique topic words.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by AI because it suggests that cosine similarity gets confused by text length, which is false. Cosine similarity specifically ignores document length by only looking at the vector directions.

exercises.prompt: You are building a search engine. How does Cosine Similarity determine if two documents are talking about the same topic using their TF-IDF representations?

options.text: It counts the total number of characters in both documents.
It measures the angle between the TF-IDF vectors of the documents.
It adds the frequencies of the most common English words.
It calculates the physical length of the paper the documents are printed on.

exercises.explain: To find how similar documents are, we turn their text into TF-IDF number lists (vectors). Then, we calculate the cosine of the angle between them. If they point in the same direction, the cosine is high (close to 1), meaning they share similar unique vocabulary!
